summary:
Neutrophils are the primary first-line defenders that rapidly engulfs bacteria upon detection due to swift recruitment and efficient phagocytic activity. While macrophages also participate in phagocytosis, they act more slowly and primarily maintain chronic responses rather than initiating immediate bacterial clearance.  

(Note: This summary focuses only on clinically significant roles related directly to bacterial engulfment during active infections.) 

End Summary.

The agent correctly identifies neutrophils as the primary cells responsible for immediately engulfing bacteria due to their rapid recruitment and potent phagocytic action at infection sites. Though macrophages contribute substantially through prolonged surveillance and tissue repair, they do so after neutrophils have initiated defense—a distinction central to understanding timely pathogen containment.
